news_summary: Today's human rights progress highlights Malawi’s proactive measures to protect people with albinism, the positive impact of narratives on aging, the recognition of early human innovation, and the celebration of LGBTQ+ history in Venezuela.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées enregistrées aujourd’hui en matière de droits de l’homme mettent en lumière les mesures proactives prises par le Malawi pour protéger les personnes atteintes d’albinisme, l’impact positif des récits sur le vieillissement, la reconnaissance des premières innovations humaines et la célébration de l’histoire de la communauté LGBTQ+ au Venezuela.
news_summary_es: Los avances de hoy en materia de derechos humanos ponen de relieve las medidas proactivas de Malaui para proteger a las personas con albinismo, el impacto positivo de los relatos sobre el envejecimiento, el reconocimiento de las primeras innovaciones humanas y la celebración de la historia del colectivo LGBTQ+ en Venezuela.

articles.title: Malawi’s New National Action Plan on Albinism
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Since 2023, Maureen Kamatu, 28, has been self-employed at a mobile money cash-transfer booth in Lilongwe. When she first started the business, she faced open discrimination from customers and neighboring vendors. “Passersby often said derogatory things about me as a person with albinism. I heard things like, ‘Is she going to be able to see well? Is she going to be able to count the money? Are you sure she can do this job?’”
                    © 2025 Samer Muscati/Human Rights Watch 
          
    


  
For years, efforts to protect the rights of people with albinism in Malawi have rightly focused on ending horrific attacks, killings, abductions, and grave tampering. These efforts remain essential as attacks continue. But safety alone is not enough.On June 19, the government announced a new National Action Plan on Persons with Albinism (2026–2030), marking an important shift toward addressing another persistent challenge: economic exclusion.The plan recognizes that poverty is both a cause and consequence of discrimination. It identifies barriers that many people with albinism face, including discrimination in hiring, unsafe and noninclusive workplaces, limited access to capital, and exclusion from social security programs.Malawi’s previous national action plan (2018–2022) focused primarily on protection from violence, justice, health, and education while largely silent on employment rights and economic discrimination.The new plan goes much further in tackling some systemic rights violations and their root causes. It includes commitments to expand vocational training, entrepreneurship, and digital literacy programs tailored to people with albinism; improve access to microfinance; promote public and private sector employment opportunities; and ensure that people with albinism, particularly women and older people, are included in social security programs.These measures were informed by consultations with organizations of people with albinism, civil society groups, the Malawi Human Rights Commission, and Human Rights Watch. Human Rights Watch provided detailed input, including recommendations related to economic inclusion and social security, many of which were included in the plan.A recent joint report by Human Rights Watch and the Africa Albinism Network documented how stigma and discrimination deny many people with albinism access to decent work and economic security.The new action plan will not, by itself, end that exclusion. Malawi’s previous action plan demonstrated how ambitious commitments can falter when funding is inadequate and implementation mechanisms are weak. The success of this new plan will depend on sustained political commitment, meaningful participation of people with albinism, and adequate resources.If fully implemented and properly funded, the plan could become a model for other countries in the region seeking to move beyond protection alone and address the social and economic inequalities that play such a central role in undermining the rights of people with albinism.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Since 2023, Maureen Kamatu, 28, has been self-employed at a mobile money cash-transfer booth in Lilongwe. When she first started the business, she faced open discrimination from customers and neighboring vendors. “Passersby often said derogatory things about me as a person with albinism. I heard things like, ‘Is she going to be able to see well? Is she going to be able to count the money? Are you sure she can do this job?’” © 2025 Samer Muscati/Human Rights Watch

For years, efforts to protect the rights of people with albinism in Malawi have rightly focused on ending horrific attacks, killings, abductions, and grave tampering. These efforts remain essential as attacks continue. But safety alone is not enough.

On June 19, the government announced a new National Action Plan on Persons with Albinism (2026–2030), marking an important shift toward addressing another persistent challenge: economic exclusion.

The plan recognizes that poverty is both a cause and consequence of discrimination. It identifies barriers that many people with albinism face, including discrimination in hiring, unsafe and noninclusive workplaces, limited access to capital, and exclusion from social security programs.

Malawi’s previous national action plan (2018–2022) focused primarily on protection from violence, justice, health, and education while largely silent on employment rights and economic discrimination.

The new plan goes much further in tackling some systemic rights violations and their root causes. It includes commitments to expand vocational training, entrepreneurship, and digital literacy programs tailored to people with albinism; improve access to microfinance; promote public and private sector employment opportunities; and ensure that people with albinism, particularly women and older people, are included in social security programs.

These measures were informed by consultations with organizations of people with albinism, civil society groups, the Malawi Human Rights Commission, and Human Rights Watch. Human Rights Watch provided detailed input, including recommendations related to economic inclusion and social security, many of which were included in the plan.

A recent joint report by Human Rights Watch and the Africa Albinism Network documented how stigma and discrimination deny many people with albinism access to decent work and economic security.

The new action plan will not, by itself, end that exclusion. Malawi’s previous action plan demonstrated how ambitious commitments can falter when funding is inadequate and implementation mechanisms are weak. The success of this new plan will depend on sustained political commitment, meaningful participation of people with albinism, and adequate resources.

If fully implemented and properly funded, the plan could become a model for other countries in the region seeking to move beyond protection alone and address the social and economic inequalities that play such a central role in undermining the rights of people with albinism.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on Malawi's new National Action Plan on Persons with Albinism (2026–2030), which addresses systemic economic exclusion and discrimination faced by people with albinism. This plan includes concrete measures such as vocational training, entrepreneurship support, microfinance access, and social security inclusion, representing a significant and meaningful policy shift with broad societal impact. The plan is well-detailed, informed by consultations with relevant stakeholders, and has the potential to serve as a regional model if properly implemented and funded.
articles.personality_title: Malawi launches new plan to improve jobs and support for people with albinism
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – People with albinism in Malawi have faced serious dangers like attacks and discrimination. Until now, the government’s efforts mainly focused on protecting them from violence and improving access to health and education.

**What happened** – On June 19, 2025, Malawi announced a new National Action Plan on Persons with Albinism for 2026 to 2030. This plan shifts focus toward fighting economic exclusion by helping people with albinism find work and access financial services. It includes expanding job training, supporting small businesses, improving digital skills, offering microloans, and including people with albinism in social security programs.

**Impact** – This plan is important because it addresses the root causes of poverty and discrimination that many people with albinism face. It goes beyond safety to help them earn a living and be part of society. For example, Maureen Kamatu, a woman with albinism, experienced discrimination when she started her own business, showing why these changes matter.

**What's next step** – The plan’s success depends on the government providing enough money and involving people with albinism in making decisions. If done well, Malawi’s approach could inspire other countries in Africa to improve economic rights for people with albinism.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Malawi’s new action plan aims to break down economic barriers for people with albinism through job training, financial support, and social inclusion, marking a major step forward in their rights and opportunities.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Malawi lance un nouveau plan pour améliorer l’emploi et le soutien aux personnes atteintes d’albinisme
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les personnes atteintes d’albinisme au Malawi ont été victimes de graves dangers comme des attaques et de la discrimination. Jusqu’à présent, les efforts du gouvernement se concentraient principalement sur leur protection contre la violence et l’amélioration de l’accès à la santé et à l’éducation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 19 juin 2025, le Malawi a annoncé un nouveau Plan d’action national pour les personnes atteintes d’albinisme pour 2026 à 2030. Ce plan met l’accent sur la lutte contre l’exclusion économique en aidant ces personnes à trouver un emploi et à accéder aux services financiers. Il prévoit d’élargir la formation professionnelle, de soutenir les petites entreprises, d’améliorer les compétences numériques, d’offrir des microcrédits et d’inclure les personnes atteintes d’albinisme dans les programmes de sécurité sociale.

**Impact** – Ce plan est important car il s’attaque aux causes profondes de la pauvreté et de la discrimination que subissent beaucoup de personnes atteintes d’albinisme. Il va au-delà de la sécurité pour les aider à gagner leur vie et à faire partie de la société. Par exemple, Maureen Kamatu, une femme atteinte d’albinisme, a subi de la discrimination en lançant sa propre entreprise, ce qui montre l’importance de ces changements.

**Prochaine étape** – Le succès du plan dépendra des financements suffisants du gouvernement et de la participation active des personnes atteintes d’albinisme aux décisions. S’il est bien mis en œuvre, l’approche du Malawi pourrait inspirer d’autres pays africains à améliorer les droits économiques des personnes atteintes d’albinisme.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le nouveau plan d’action du Malawi vise à lever les barrières économiques pour les personnes atteintes d’albinisme grâce à la formation, au soutien financier et à l’inclusion sociale, marquant une avancée majeure pour leurs droits et opportunités.
articles.personality_title_es: Malawi lanza un nuevo plan para mejorar empleos y apoyo a personas con albinismo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las personas con albinismo en Malawi han enfrentado peligros graves como ataques y discriminación. Hasta ahora, los esfuerzos del gobierno se centraban principalmente en protegerlas de la violencia y mejorar el acceso a la salud y la educación.

**Qué pasó** – El 19 de junio de 2025, Malawi anunció un nuevo Plan de Acción Nacional para Personas con Albinismo para 2026 a 2030. Este plan cambia el enfoque hacia combatir la exclusión económica ayudando a estas personas a encontrar trabajo y acceder a servicios financieros. Incluye ampliar la capacitación laboral, apoyar emprendimientos, mejorar habilidades digitales, ofrecer microcréditos e incluir a las personas con albinismo en programas de seguridad social.

**Impacto** – Este plan es importante porque aborda las causas profundas de la pobreza y discriminación que muchas personas con albinismo enfrentan. Va más allá de la seguridad para ayudarles a ganarse la vida y ser parte de la sociedad. Por ejemplo, Maureen Kamatu, una mujer con albinismo, sufrió discriminación al iniciar su propio negocio, mostrando por qué estos cambios son necesarios.

**Próximo paso** – El éxito del plan dependerá de que el gobierno asigne suficientes fondos y de la participación activa de las personas con albinismo en las decisiones. Si se implementa bien, el plan de Malawi podría inspirar a otros países de África a mejorar los derechos económicos de las personas con albinismo.

**Resumen en una frase** – El nuevo plan de Malawi busca eliminar barreras económicas para personas con albinismo mediante capacitación, apoyo financiero e inclusión social, marcando un avance importante en sus derechos y oportunidades.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy, open market stall with a glowing, translucent white chameleon perched confidently on the counter, symbolizing albinism and resilience, surrounded by diverse, colorful woven baskets representing economic opportunity and inclusion, set against a soft, natural palette of earth tones and gentle sunlight filtering through an open sky.

articles.title: How the stories we tell about ageing shape our future
articles.summary: The way societies talk about ageing can shape everything from public policy to people's own expectations of later life, the United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE) said on Wednesday in a new report that calls  for shifting away from narratives that portray older persons as a burden.
articles.content:
For many people, ageing is accompanied by familiar messages: slow down, your best years are behind you.

Far from harmless clichés, these assumptions shape how societies view older persons and how people see themselves as they grow older.

In many cultures, ageing is still associated with decline, dependency and withdrawal from public life. Such perceptions can influence attitudes, policies and opportunities, while also leading people to internalise ageist beliefs that ultimately limit wellbeing.

‘Public narratives matter’

The latest UNECE Policy Brief on Ageing, Changing the Narrative on Ageing and Older Persons, argues that the way societies talk about ageing has far-reaching consequences.

Across much of the UNECE region – which spans the continent as well as a handful of countries in North America and Asia – population ageing is frequently framed as a challenge, burden or even a looming crisis.

The brief calls for balanced, evidence-based and age-inclusive narratives that better reflect the opportunities and diversity associated with longer lives. It also presents examples of actions taken across the UNECE region to promote more positive portrayals of ageing and offers practical recommendations for policymakers.

Beyond the stereotypes

People are living longer and, in many cases, healthier lives. Millions of older persons continue to work, volunteer, care for family members and contribute to their communities.

In the European Union alone, around 41 million people aged 55 to 64 are active in the labour market, with participation increasing significantly over the past decade. Older persons also make substantial unpaid contributions through caregiving and volunteering, strengthening families, communities and local economies.

This contribution is increasingly recognised as an economic opportunity rather than a challenge. A recent World Economic Forum report describes longevity as "one of the most consequential, addressable and underestimated potential drivers of economic growth" for countries with both younger and older populations.

The report argues that coordinated approaches linking longer, healthier lives with greater financial resilience could generate multi-trillion-dollar economic opportunities in the years ahead.

However, these realities are often overshadowed by crisis-oriented language focusing on pressures on pensions, healthcare systems and public finances.

"When ageing is viewed primarily through the lens of dependency and cost, policy debates can become narrowly focused on short-term challenges," the brief notes.

The report warns that terms such as "demographic time bomb" or "silver tsunami" can reinforce stereotypes and narrow public discussion about how societies can adapt to demographic change.

The hidden impact of ageism

Research has shown that age-related stereotypes can have tangible effects on health, wellbeing and social inclusion. According to UNECE, changing the narrative is therefore not simply a communications exercise but a necessary step towards tackling ageism and promoting participation throughout the life course.

The brief also highlights that ageism affects people of all ages and can undermine trust and solidarity between generations.

Turning words into action

To help countries reshape public discourse on ageing, the policy brief proposes a practical framework for change.

Recommendations include developing a clear and realistic vision of ageing, engaging older persons in decision-making, highlighting diverse lived experiences and communicating consistently across institutions and media.

Crucially, UNECE stresses that narrative change must be backed by policy action.

Building societies for all ages

As populations continue to age, UNECE argues that the challenge is not ageing itself but how societies respond to it.

By portraying ageing as a shared achievement and opportunity rather than a crisis, governments can broaden policy choices, strengthen solidarity between generations and build support for forward-looking solutions.

Across much of the UNECE region, population ageing is frequently portrayed as a “burden” or “demographic crisis”, while older persons are often depicted as dependent or withdrawn from society. According to the brief, such narratives are misaligned with reality.

The message aligns with the goals of the UN Decade of Healthy Ageing (2021–2030), a global initiative that aims to ensure everyone has the opportunity to add life to years, wherever they live.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant societal shift advocated by the UNECE to change negative narratives about ageing, promoting age-inclusive policies and combating ageism. This has broad positive implications for social inclusion, wellbeing, and intergenerational solidarity across many countries, backed by detailed context and actionable recommendations.
articles.personality_title: UNECE calls for positive stories about ageing to shape better futures
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many societies often describe ageing as a time of decline and burden, which affects how older people are treated and how they see themselves. These negative views can influence public policies and limit opportunities for older adults.

**What happened** – On Wednesday, the United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE) released a report urging a change in how ageing is talked about. The report encourages moving away from negative words like “burden” or “crisis” and towards stories that recognize the contributions and diversity of older people.

**Impact** – This shift is important because millions of older adults continue to work, volunteer, and support their communities. For example, in the European Union, around 41 million people aged 55 to 64 are active workers. Recognizing these facts can help create better policies and reduce ageism, which harms people’s health and social inclusion.

**What's next step** – The UNECE suggests that countries adopt clear, positive messages about ageing, involve older people in decisions, and ensure consistent communication across media and governments. These changes should be supported by policies that promote inclusion and respect for all ages.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Changing the way we talk about ageing can lead to fairer policies and a society that values people at every stage of life.
articles.personality_title_fr: La CEE-ONU appelle à des récits positifs sur le vieillissement pour un avenir meilleur
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans de nombreuses sociétés, le vieillissement est souvent décrit comme une période de déclin et de fardeau, ce qui influence la manière dont les personnes âgées sont traitées et perçoivent elles-mêmes leur vie. Ces visions négatives peuvent affecter les politiques publiques et limiter les opportunités.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Mercredi, la Commission économique pour l’Europe des Nations Unies (CEE-ONU) a publié un rapport appelant à changer la manière dont on parle du vieillissement. Le rapport encourage à éviter les mots négatifs comme « fardeau » ou « crise » et à raconter des histoires qui reconnaissent les contributions et la diversité des personnes âgées.

**Impact** – Ce changement est important car des millions de personnes âgées continuent de travailler, de faire du bénévolat et de soutenir leurs communautés. Par exemple, dans l’Union européenne, environ 41 millions de personnes âgées de 55 à 64 ans sont actives sur le marché du travail. Reconnaître ces faits peut aider à créer de meilleures politiques et réduire l’âgisme, qui nuit à la santé et à l’inclusion sociale.

**Prochaine étape** – La CEE-ONU suggère que les pays adoptent des messages clairs et positifs sur le vieillissement, impliquent les personnes âgées dans les décisions, et assurent une communication cohérente dans les médias et les gouvernements. Ces changements doivent être soutenus par des politiques favorisant l’inclusion et le respect de tous les âges.

**En une phrase** – Changer la façon dont nous parlons du vieillissement peut conduire à des politiques plus justes et à une société qui valorise les personnes à chaque étape de la vie.
articles.personality_title_es: La CEP-ONU pide relatos positivos sobre el envejecimiento para un futuro mejor
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En muchas sociedades, el envejecimiento suele describirse como un tiempo de declive y carga, lo que afecta cómo se trata a las personas mayores y cómo ellas mismas se ven. Estas ideas negativas pueden influir en las políticas públicas y limitar las oportunidades.

**Qué pasó** – El miércoles, la Comisión Económica para Europa de las Naciones Unidas (CEP-ONU) publicó un informe que pide cambiar la forma en que se habla del envejecimiento. El informe alienta a evitar palabras negativas como «carga» o «crisis» y a contar historias que reconozcan las contribuciones y la diversidad de las personas mayores.

**Impacto** – Este cambio es importante porque millones de personas mayores siguen trabajando, haciendo voluntariado y apoyando a sus comunidades. Por ejemplo, en la Unión Europea, alrededor de 41 millones de personas de 55 a 64 años están activas en el mercado laboral. Reconocer estos hechos puede ayudar a crear mejores políticas y reducir el edadismo, que afecta la salud y la inclusión social.

**Próximo paso** – La CEP-ONU sugiere que los países adopten mensajes claros y positivos sobre el envejecimiento, involucren a las personas mayores en las decisiones y aseguren una comunicación coherente en medios y gobiernos. Estos cambios deben ir acompañados de políticas que promuevan la inclusión y el respeto a todas las edades.

**Una frase clave** – Cambiar la manera en que hablamos sobre el envejecimiento puede llevar a políticas más justas y a una sociedad que valora a las personas en todas las etapas de la vida.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a blooming tree with strong, intertwining branches shaped like diverse, active silhouettes representing older adults engaging in various roles—working, volunteering, caregiving—set against a softly glowing horizon with warm, natural earth tones, symbolizing growth, contribution, and the positive, interconnected journey of ageing.

articles.title: Early humans were bringing fire into caves 1.8 million years ago
articles.summary: A new study suggests early humans were using fire in South Africa’s Wonderwerk Cave as far back as 1.79 million years ago. Researchers found burned bones deep inside the cave, where natural wildfires could not have reached, indicating that fire was likely carried in and maintained by human ancestors. The discovery pushes back the timeline for fire use and reveals surprisingly sophisticated behavior long before humans could create fire on demand.
articles.content:
Scientists have uncovered new evidence that early human ancestors were using fire in South Africa's Wonderwerk Cave between 1.07 and 1.79 million years ago. The discovery pushes back one of the earliest known records of fire use linked to hominins and offers fresh clues about how our ancestors first learned to harness fire.

Using a newly developed technique that can detect signs of burning in fossilized bones, researchers identified repeated evidence of fire deep inside the cave. Because these traces were found far beyond the reach of natural wildfires, the findings suggest that early humans were deliberately bringing naturally occurring fire into the cave and keeping it burning.

The research was carried out through an ongoing collaboration led by Dr. Liora Kolska Horwitz of the Hebrew University of Jerusalem's National Natural History Collections (co-director of the Wonderwerk Cave project with Prof Michael Chazan, University of Toronto) together with an international team of scientists from Spain, Argentina, Canada, USA, South Africa, Portugal and Israel. The project combines archaeology, paleontology, geology, and other scientific approaches to investigate one of the most important developments in human evolution: the use of fire.

Earlier Evidence of Fire Use

The new study builds on earlier work at Wonderwerk Cave, located in South Africa's Kalahari Desert. In 2012, members of the research team reported evidence of fire dating to about ~1 million years ago (published by members of the team in 2012 in PNAS), which was considered the oldest known evidence of intentional fire use anywhere in the world.

Continued excavations and analysis have now extended that timeline. Researchers identified traces of fire use in archaeological deposits dating from 1.07 to 1.79 million years ago, making Wonderwerk Cave one of the oldest known sites associated with hominin fire use. The findings, published in PLOS One, provide new insight into how ancient human ancestors may have interacted with fire long before they learned how to produce it themselves.

Fire offered many advantages, including warmth, protection from predators, light after dark, and eventually the ability to cook food. Even so, determining when humans first began using fire has remained one of archaeology's most difficult questions.

"Evidence of fire from such ancient sites is often subtle and difficult to detect," said the Dr. Kolska Horwitz. "Our study provides new tools for identifying traces of ancient burning and reveals that fire was repeatedly present deep inside Wonderwerk Cave."

New Technique Detects Burned Fossil Bones

The study also introduces a new approach based on the light-emitting properties of burned bone.

When exposed to specific wavelengths of light, bones that have experienced intense heating produce a distinctive glow. Researchers combined this non-destructive luminescence method with established chemical analyses, allowing them to identify burned animal bones with a high level of confidence.

The technique is portable, non-invasive, and can be used on large fossil collections without causing damage.

To test the method, the team examined hundreds of tiny fossil bones left behind by owls that once roosted inside the cave. Because these remains accumulated naturally over time, they provide an independent, non-anthropogenic record of past events preserved on the cave floor.

Fire Deep Inside Wonderwerk Cave

The researchers discovered clear evidence of burning within an archaeological layer associated with early Acheulean artifacts, likely linked to Homo erectus. The burned remains were found approximately 30 meters inside the cave, far beyond the area that could have been affected by natural wildfires. They were also located in a layer that lacked guano deposits, ruling out spontaneous combustion as an explanation.

The evidence does not suggest that these early humans were capable of creating fire whenever they wanted. Instead, the findings indicate that they likely collected fire from natural sources, such as lightning strikes or wildfires on the African savanna.

According to the researchers, these ancient humans brought fire into the cave on multiple occasions and maintained it for a period before it eventually went out. The team also suggested that owl pellets may have served as fuel, which could explain why the tiny rodent bones contained within them show signs of burning.

Even so, the ability to transport fire and keep it burning inside a cave represents a major behavioral milestone.

"These discoveries show that early humans were not simply passive observers of natural fires," Dr. Kolska Horwitz explained. "They were actively engaging with fire and incorporating it into their lives."

A New Window Into the Origins of Fire

In addition to extending the timeline of fire use, the study provides archaeologists with a valuable new tool for exploring when and how humans first began using fire.

As scientists apply this technique to archaeological sites around the world, it could help answer long-standing questions about the origins and evolution of one of the most transformative technologies in human history.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that pushes back the timeline for early human use of fire, revealing sophisticated behavior that marks a major milestone in human evolution. This finding has broad significance for understanding human history and evolution, with substantial context and detail provided about the discovery, methods, and implications.
articles.personality_title: Early humans brought fire into caves nearly 1.8 million years ago
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered when early humans first started using fire. Fire helped people stay warm, cook food, and keep safe from animals. Evidence of early fire use is rare and hard to find.

**What happened** – Researchers studying Wonderwerk Cave in South Africa found burned bones deep inside the cave, where wildfires could not reach. Using a new light-based technique, they discovered signs of fire use dating back between 1.07 and 1.79 million years ago. This means early humans, likely Homo erectus, were carrying and keeping fire burning inside the cave.

**Impact** – This discovery pushes back the known timeline of fire use by humans and shows that early humans were more skilled than previously thought. They didn’t make fire themselves yet but knew how to collect it from natural sources like lightning and keep it alive. This behavior marks an important step in human evolution.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to use this new method to look for fire evidence at other ancient sites worldwide. This could help us learn more about how and when humans learned to control fire, a key part of our history.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Early humans in South Africa carried and maintained natural fire inside caves nearly 1.8 million years ago, showing advanced behavior long before they could create fire themselves.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les premiers humains apportaient le feu dans les grottes il y a près de 1,8 million d'années
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps à savoir quand les premiers humains ont commencé à utiliser le feu. Le feu aidait à se réchauffer, cuisiner et se protéger des animaux. Les preuves d’une utilisation ancienne du feu sont rares et difficiles à trouver.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs étudiant la grotte Wonderwerk en Afrique du Sud ont trouvé des os brûlés profondément dans la grotte, là où les feux de forêt naturels ne pouvaient pas atteindre. Grâce à une nouvelle technique basée sur la lumière, ils ont découvert des traces d’utilisation du feu datant de 1,07 à 1,79 million d’années. Cela montre que les premiers humains, probablement Homo erectus, transportaient et maintenaient le feu dans la grotte.

**Impact** – Cette découverte repousse la date connue de l’utilisation du feu par les humains et montre que les premiers humains étaient plus habiles que ce que l’on pensait. Ils ne fabriquaient pas encore le feu eux-mêmes, mais savaient le collecter dans la nature, par exemple après un éclair, et le garder allumé. Ce comportement est une étape importante dans l’évolution humaine.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont utiliser cette nouvelle méthode pour chercher des preuves de feu sur d’autres sites anciens dans le monde. Cela pourrait nous aider à mieux comprendre comment et quand les humains ont appris à maîtriser le feu, un élément clé de notre histoire.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Il y a près de 1,8 million d’années, les premiers humains en Afrique du Sud transportaient et entretenaient du feu naturel dans des grottes, montrant un comportement avancé bien avant de pouvoir créer le feu eux-mêmes.
articles.personality_title_es: Los primeros humanos llevaban fuego a las cuevas hace casi 1,8 millones de años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han querido saber por mucho tiempo cuándo comenzaron los primeros humanos a usar el fuego. El fuego ayudaba a mantenerse calientes, cocinar comida y protegerse de animales. Las pruebas de uso temprano del fuego son escasas y difíciles de encontrar.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores que estudiaban la cueva Wonderwerk en Sudáfrica encontraron huesos quemados en lo profundo de la cueva, donde no podían llegar los incendios naturales. Usando una nueva técnica basada en luz, descubrieron señales de uso de fuego entre 1,07 y 1,79 millones de años atrás. Esto muestra que los primeros humanos, probablemente Homo erectus, llevaban y mantenían el fuego dentro de la cueva.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento adelanta la fecha conocida del uso del fuego por humanos y muestra que los primeros humanos eran más hábiles de lo que se pensaba. Aún no sabían crear fuego, pero sí cómo recogerlo de fuentes naturales como rayos y mantenerlo encendido. Este comportamiento es un paso importante en la evolución humana.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar este nuevo método para buscar evidencias de fuego en otros sitios antiguos del mundo. Esto podría ayudar a entender mejor cómo y cuándo los humanos aprendieron a controlar el fuego, una parte clave de nuestra historia.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Hace casi 1,8 millones de años, los primeros humanos en Sudáfrica llevaban y mantenían fuego natural dentro de cuevas, mostrando un comportamiento avanzado mucho antes de poder crear fuego por sí mismos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing campfire deep inside a dimly lit ancient cave, with charred animal bones scattered near the fire’s base and faint silhouettes of early stone tools partially embedded in the cave floor, all rendered in natural earthy tones of ochre, brown, and soft amber light.

articles.title: Three years of Pride: Santiago Méndez’s visual archive of queer Venezuela
articles.summary: An interview with Venezuelan visual artist and photographer Santiago Méndez, who has exhaustively documente queerness and Pride in Caracas for the last three years.
articles.content:
This post is part of Global Voices’ June 2026 Spotlight series, “Gender Diversity.” This series offers insight into gender diversity and how it is being threatened, protected, and preserved around the world. You can support this coverage by donating here.

For the past three years, photographer and visual artist Santiago Méndez Arvelaez has been documenting Pride celebrations in Venezuela’s capital city, creating a powerful visual record of queer life, visibility, and resistance.

Born in Caracas in 1998, Méndez explores identity through photography, research, and multimedia visual practices rooted in Venezuelan popular culture. Méndez’s work stands out for its sustained attention to the country’s cultural and social complexities. As part of a vibrant tradition of documentary photography in Venezuela, he is establishing himself as one of the artists creating some of the most compelling archives of communities that remain largely underreported in mainstream narratives.

His work approaches documentary photography with an anthropological perspective, capturing a country shaped by fashion, urban style, Afro-descendant traditions, and popular art, while tracing the connections between contemporary expression and long-standing cultural histories.

Gabriela Mesones Rojo (GMR): Why do you think documenting queer experiences in Venezuela is so important right now?

Santiago Méndez (SM): Venezuela is often explained through politics, migration, collapse. I grew up inside that and I have never experienced my country beyond Chavismo. When everything is reduced to crisis, other stories disappear, but the ability to be yourself in public, love openly, and be recognized says a lot about the country you live in. Venezuela still has no marriage equality, no recognition for same sex couples, and trans legal gender recognition remains blocked in practice. The Venezuelan Observatory of LGBTIQ+ Violence recorded 461 cases of violence and discrimination in 2023, affecting 394 people, and five deaths. Queer archives prove that people were here. It leaves evidence of our existence.

GMR: The last three years have shown deep political changes in the country. What changes have you noticed in Caracas’ Pride over the years, whether in participation, visibility, emotions, or political atmosphere?

SM: In 2023 I felt a huge energy in the street. Pride felt crowded, loud, almost unreal. There was joy everywhere, and I believe in that joy. Still, I kept thinking about the pain carried under the music. Existing together in the street is political, even when people do not name it that way. 2024 feels important because it was a hinge year. I went with my mom and friends. The march happened three weeks before the presidential election, and the slogan was ‘Our existence is not public indecency.’ It came after 33 people were detained during a 2023 police raid at a Valencia LGBTIQ+ venue. They were charged with public indecency, criminal association and noise pollution, in a case organizations denounced as discriminatory criminalization in a private queer space. By 2025 the mood felt different. Smaller. More careful. Less formally accompanied. Some organizations reportedly participated discreetly. The landscape was marked by arrests and exile after the 2024 presidential election. Pride Month 2026 began with reports that at least 30 men were held in a police operation at a Barquisimeto LGBTIQ+ venue, after officers allegedly accused them of ‘committing the crime of homosexuality.’ Homosexuality is not illegal in Venezuela, but police still behave as if it should be. For many LGBTIQ+ Venezuelans, one of the biggest fears is an encounter with the authorities. I cannot describe these years as progress. There’s visibility, but also fear. My photos live in that tension.

GMR: As a queer photographer and visual artist, how do you think about representation and ethics when photographing queer experiences? What responsibilities come with creating queer visual archives?

SM: Being queer changes how I photograph these spaces. I recognize parts of myself in the people I photograph. That closeness does not give me ownership over their stories. In Venezuela, a published image can expose someone to family, work, or police. Public space does not erase risk. So I work with doubt. Some photographs stay unpublished. Some faces stay out of the archive. I am skeptical of ‘photographer heroism.’ Photography can preserve, inform, and make people pay attention, but it does not replace action, context, or material change. Without research, there is misunderstanding. Without relationship, there is extraction. Representation is not about making the community look perfect. The Venezuelan queer community carries the same prejudices, desires and contradictions as the rest of the country. I am part of that reality too, so I approach it with honesty instead of judgment.

GMR: Why is photography fundamental to preserving queer cultural memory? What does photography preserve that other forms of storytelling cannot?

SM: Queer photography matters because much of our history was hidden, mocked, or told by people who did not love us. In Venezuela, the archive is full of gaps. Many queer lives were photographed only through shame, scandal, police language, medical language and meme language. The Venezuelan Observatory of LGBTIQ+ Violence reported that at least 93 trans people were murdered in Venezuela between 2008 and 2023, all of them trans women. That number changes the meaning of celebration. I want someone in the future to see these photographs and understand that queer Venezuelans existed in public before the country recognized them. We love here. We make style here. We make noise here. We belong here, even when belonging was denied.

GMR: What has photographing queer life taught you about Venezuela?
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the important work of Venezuelan photographer Santiago Méndez in documenting queer life and Pride in Caracas, creating a vital visual archive that preserves queer cultural memory and visibility in a challenging political and social environment. This work has broad significance for human rights by raising awareness, preserving marginalized histories, and fostering recognition and resistance against discrimination and violence faced by the LGBTQ+ community in Venezuela.
articles.personality_title: Photographer Santiago Méndez captures three years of Pride in Caracas
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Venezuela, many stories about queer people are often overlooked because of political and social challenges. Pride celebrations, which show support and visibility for LGBTQ+ communities, have faced difficulties, including police raids and discrimination.

**What happened** – Over the past three years, Santiago Méndez, a photographer and visual artist from Caracas, has been documenting Pride events and queer life in Venezuela’s capital. His photos show the joy, struggle, and strength of the community, creating an important record of their existence and experiences.

**Impact** – Méndez’s work is special because it offers a close, honest look at queer culture in a country where many LGBTQ+ rights are not recognized. His photos help preserve stories that might otherwise be lost, especially as the community faces violence and fear. By capturing these moments, he shows that queer Venezuelans have always been part of the country’s culture.

**What's next step** – Méndez continues to build this visual archive carefully, respecting the privacy and safety of the people he photographs. His work may inspire others to document and share queer histories, helping to raise awareness and support for LGBTQ+ rights in Venezuela and beyond.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Santiago Méndez’s photography creates a vital record of queer life and Pride in Caracas, preserving stories of visibility and resilience amid challenges.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le photographe Santiago Méndez immortalise trois ans de Pride à Caracas
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au Venezuela, les histoires des personnes queer sont souvent ignorées à cause des difficultés politiques et sociales. Les célébrations Pride, qui soutiennent la communauté LGBTQ+, rencontrent des obstacles, comme des raids policiers et de la discrimination.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Depuis trois ans, Santiago Méndez, photographe et artiste visuel de Caracas, documente les événements Pride et la vie queer dans la capitale. Ses photos montrent la joie, les luttes et la force de cette communauté, créant un important témoignage de leur existence.

**Impact** – Le travail de Méndez est unique car il offre un regard proche et sincère sur la culture queer dans un pays où les droits LGBTQ+ sont peu reconnus. Ses photos aident à préserver des histoires qui pourraient disparaître, surtout face à la violence et la peur. Elles montrent que les queer vénézuéliens ont toujours fait partie de la culture du pays.

**Prochaine étape** – Méndez continue de construire cette archive visuelle avec soin, en respectant la sécurité des personnes photographiées. Son travail pourrait encourager d’autres à documenter et partager l’histoire queer, aidant à sensibiliser et soutenir les droits LGBTQ+ au Venezuela et ailleurs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La photographie de Santiago Méndez crée un témoignage essentiel de la vie queer et de Pride à Caracas, préservant des récits de visibilité et de résistance malgré les défis.
articles.personality_title_es: El fotógrafo Santiago Méndez registra tres años de Pride en Caracas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Venezuela, las historias de las personas queer suelen ser ignoradas debido a problemas políticos y sociales. Las celebraciones del Pride, que apoyan a la comunidad LGBTQ+, enfrentan dificultades como redadas policiales y discriminación.

**Qué pasó** – Durante los últimos tres años, Santiago Méndez, fotógrafo y artista visual de Caracas, ha estado documentando los eventos del Pride y la vida queer en la capital. Sus fotos muestran la alegría, la lucha y la fuerza de esta comunidad, creando un registro importante de su existencia.

**Impacto** – El trabajo de Méndez es especial porque ofrece una mirada cercana y honesta a la cultura queer en un país donde los derechos LGBTQ+ no están reconocidos. Sus fotos ayudan a preservar historias que podrían perderse, especialmente frente a la violencia y el miedo. Demuestran que los venezolanos queer siempre han sido parte de la cultura del país.

**Próximo paso** – Méndez sigue construyendo este archivo visual con cuidado, respetando la privacidad y seguridad de las personas fotografiadas. Su trabajo puede inspirar a otros a documentar y compartir historias queer, ayudando a crear conciencia y apoyar los derechos LGBTQ+ en Venezuela y más allá.

**Resumen en una frase** – La fotografía de Santiago Méndez crea un registro vital de la vida queer y el Pride en Caracas, preservando historias de visibilidad y resistencia ante los desafíos.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a colorful urban street scene in Caracas at dusk, featuring abstract silhouettes of diverse people joyfully gathered in a peaceful Pride celebration; the scene includes subtle references to Venezuelan culture through traditional patterns woven into flowing fabrics and Afro-descendant-inspired motifs subtly integrated into the architecture, while soft, warm natural tones evoke resilience, visibility, and quiet strength amidst an atmosphere of cautious hope.
